main_sections.title: Words
main_sections.poem_text:
If words could satisfy the heart,
The heart might find less care;
But words, like summer birds, depart,
And leave but empty air.

The heart, a pilgrim upon earth,
Finds often, when it needs,
That words are of as little worth
As just so many weeds.

A little said, and truly said,
Can deeper joy impart
Than hosts of words, which reach the head,
But never touch the heart.

The voice that wins its sunny way,
A lonely home to cheer,
Hath oft the fewest words to say;
But, oh! those few, how dear!

If words could satisfy the chest,
The world might hold a feast;
But words, when summoned to the test,
Oft satisfy the least!

Like plants that make a gaudy show,
All blossom to the root;
But whose poor nature cannot grow,
One particle of fruit!

Charles Swain
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: The poem by Charles Swain meditates on the paradox of language: words, despite their abundance, are unreliable vessels for the heart's deepest needs. Using the central simile of summer birds that depart and leave empty air, Swain captures the transience and inadequacy of hollow speech. The heart is figured as a pilgrim — restless, searching — who discovers that words, when most needed, are as useless as weeds. The poem's most poignant claim is that a few sincere, truly-spoken words carry more joy than a torrent of empty ones. The dominant devices are simile (words like summer birds; words like weeds; words like gaudy plants), metaphor (heart as pilgrim; words as feast/fruit), and hyperbole (the world might hold a feast). The rhyme scheme is ABAB throughout, and the regular iambic metre gives the poem its hymn-like rhythm, reinforcing the idea that meaningful communication — like music — is a matter of quality over quantity.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Appreciation + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Why are words important? Can we communicate without words? How? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
Use the given sentences as clues to find words from the grid and fill the blanks. One example has been done for you (summoned). Find horizontal and vertical answers.

question_bank.stem: Horizontal 1: We eat food to _______ our hunger.
Horizontal 2: The train will _______ from the station at 5.00 p.m.
Horizontal 3: The gardener was removing the _______ to clean the flower beds.
Vertical 4: The view from the top of the hill was _______ the difficult climb.
Vertical 5: The herbs and spices _______ flavour to food.
Vertical 6: Flowers _______ in spring.
Vertical 7: We enjoyed a delicious _______ after the ceremony.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks with one word from the poem. One example has been done for you (satisfy). Complete the paragraph summarising the poem's ideas.

question_bank.stem: Words are compared to summer birds who 1. __________, leaving nothing behind.
The heart, is equated to a 2. __________ who finds that words are as worthless as 3. __________ when needed.
He feels that a few sincere words can bring more 4. __________ than many meaningless ones.
A voice that brings happiness to a 5. __________ place does not say much, but the few words it does, are very precious.
If words could satisfy us, the 6. __________ would celebrate but words often fail to do that.
The poem ends on the note that empty words may look impressive with lots of flowers, but they cannot produce anything valuable, like a 7. __________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us appreciate the poem. Read the poem again. Find any four sets of rhyming words and also write the rhyme scheme of the poem.
Read the following phrases and sentences taken from the poem. Identify the poetic device in each of them and explain what the poet wants to communicate through their usage.

question_bank.stem: (i) But words, like summer birds, depart…
(ii) heart, a pilgrim upon earth…
(iii) words are of as little worth / As just so many weeds
(iv) If words could satisfy the chest … Oft satisfy the least!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following phrases and sentences taken from the poem. Identify the poetic device in each of them and explain what the poet wants to communicate through their usage (continued).

question_bank.stem: (v) The world might hold a feast…
(vi) Like plants that make a gaudy show / All blossom to the root
(vii) But whose poor nature cannot grow / One particle of fruit!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Which words are repeated in the poem? Why does the poet use repetition?
The stanzas 4, 5, and 6 end with exclamation marks. Choose the option that displays the emotions being expressed through them.

question_bank.stem: Choose from: (i) 4.disillusionment 5.admiration 6.frustration  (ii) 4.frustration 5.admiration 6.disillusionment  (iii) 4.admiration 5.frustration 6.disillusionment  (iv) 4.frustration 5.disillusionment 6.admiration

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify examples of hyperbole from the given lines: 'If words could satisfy the chest, / The world might hold a feast.' Work in pairs. Complete the sentences given below with hyperboles. Use the hints given in the brackets.

question_bank.stem: I have ______________ things to do on this weekend. (lots of/tonnes of)
The player missed the basket by ______________. (a mile/ an inch)
My mother is so tired that she can sleep for a ______________. (night/decade)
I will be back in ______________. (five minutes/two seconds)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Rhythm gives a lyrical quality to a poem. The metrical form in the first four lines is given below (stressed syllables underlined). Now, listen to the teacher read the poem. As you listen, underline the syllables that are stressed in all the lines of the poem. Share your answers with the teacher.
Read the following lines and answer the questions. Extract 1: 'The heart, a pilgrim upon earth, / Finds often, when it needs, / That words are of as little worth / As just so many weeds.'

question_bank.stem: Why has the poet referred to the heart as 'a pilgrim'?
When would a heart 'need' words?
Complete the sentence: The words are like weeds because __________.
Mention two emotions the heart might be experiencing when it finds words to be of 'little worth'.
What do these lines suggest about the nature of communication?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following lines and answer the questions. Extract 2: 'If words could satisfy the chest, / The world might hold a feast; / But words, when summoned to the test, / Oft satisfy the least!'

question_bank.stem: How can words 'satisfy the chest'?
How can words be 'summoned to the test'?
What does 'the world' holding 'a feast' imply?
Complete the sentence: The poet mentions that words satisfy the least because __________.
Select the word that does not mean the same as 'oft': A. always  B. usually  C. frequently  D. often

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What is the comparison that the poet draws between words and 'empty air'?
According to the poet meaningful words are more precious than a lot of them. Explain.
Do you agree that the poet presents contrasting ideas related to 'words' in the poem? If yes, why? If no, why not?
The theme of loneliness hovers over the poem. Support this statement with examples from the text.
How does the poet convey the superficial nature of words? What ought to be done to address this?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The poet has used some figurative phrases. Match the phrases in Column 1 (satisfy the heart; depart and leave but empty air; hosts of words; never touch the heart; wins its sunny way; plants that cannot grow fruit) with their correct meanings in Column 2. Use each phrase in a sentence of your own.

question_bank.stem: satisfy the heart — match meaning
depart and leave but empty air — match meaning
hosts of words — match meaning
never touch the heart — match meaning
wins its sunny way — match meaning
plants that cannot grow fruit — match meaning

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Create a 'Word Map' for each of the words from the poem: depart, pilgrim, cheer, word, sunny, satisfy, heart. Include: meaning (in your own words), synonyms, antonyms, sentence using the word, a quick sketch or symbol. One example has been done for you (cheer).

question_bank.stem: Word map for: depart
Word map for: pilgrim
Word map for: word
Word map for: sunny
Word map for: satisfy
Word map for: heart

listening.transcript_text: A conversation between a girl (Sonali) and a boy (Vivek). Vivek mentions an inter-school declamation contest next week on the topic 'Use of English Language for Communication'. Sonali confirms she registered early. She argues the topic is engaging because English is used worldwide for informal and formal communication — sharing information, expressing ideas, relating experiences. Vivek adds that effective communication requires organising thoughts well and choosing appropriate words. Sonali agrees, noting many people face difficulty because English is not their mother tongue and hesitate due to insufficient vocabulary and grammar knowledge. She recommends developing vocabulary and grammar to communicate with confidence, and says this also shows in body language. When Vivek asks how, she explains that communication happens not only with words but also with gestures and postures, so body language must be worked on. Vivek says he will find a book on body language from the library's reference section. Sonali confirms he will find it there.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a girl and a boy. As you listen, mark four statements from 1–6 that are true.

question_bank.stem: 1. The girl was excited to participate in the declamation contest.
2. The boy was doubtful about the suitability of the topic chosen for the contest.
3. The girl explained that people prefer to keep their personal experiences to themselves.
4. The boy was unaware of the role played by body language in reflecting the speaker's confidence.
5. According to the girl, body language had an insignificant role to play in communication.
6. The girl had issued the book on body language earlier from the library.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following quotations (by Gautama Buddha, Swami Vivekananda, Mahatma Gandhi, Kalpana Chawla, Walt Whitman, Emily Dickinson, Homer, Martin Luther King Jr.) and select the one you like the most. Explain the quotation and share the reason for your choice with your classmates and teacher. You may talk about a connected personal experience too. Use verbal cues: I chose___ because...; I selected ___ since...; I decided on ___due to...; I picked ___as...; I settled for ___ because...

Write an essay on any one quotation from the speaking activity. Follow the guidelines: (1) Introduction — mention the quotation and speaker, briefly state reason for appeal, state purpose of essay; (2 & 3) Body paragraphs — each explains a specific point with topic sentence, evidence/examples, transition words; (4) Counterargument — identify a possible criticism and refute it; (5) Conclusion — summarise main points and end with a lasting impression.

In groups of six, follow the instructions to play the 'Non-verbal' game. Each student writes or makes an emoji with an expression on one slip and a gesture on the other. Jumble the slips and keep them upside down. Pick the slips in turns and enact it in 30 seconds. The person who can identify the expression or gesture first, gets 10 points. Continue for two rounds and find the Non-verbal Champion. (This game is like Dumb Charades.)

Read about: The French priest Charles-Michel de l'Épée (1712–89) is recognised as 'The Father of Sign Language and Deaf Education'. Discuss the significance of sign language as a form of non-verbal communication.

Read and enjoy the poem 'Weigh Your Words' by E. F. Hayward: 'If we would "weigh" the words we say, / And speak but those we mean, / We'd save a lot of talk each day, / And keep our conscience clean. / The idle words we often speak, / And promises we make, / If given credence, by the weak, / May cause some heart to ache. / We never know the pain and sting / An idle word may start; / We never can erase one thing, / We've written on a heart. / 'Tis best to "weigh" each sentence well, / And as I've said before, / Tho "we'd not have so much to tell, / 'Twould mean a whole lot more.' — E. F. Hayward
